1: anachthentes de apespasmen ap autōn (anachthentes de apespasmen ap' autōn): 'after tearing ourselves away from them, we set sail'. Apospaō (pull away/tear away) is the word for forcible separation — the departure from Miletus is not a clean send-off but an emotionally costly severance. The 'we' narrator is present throughout ch. 21. The coastal route — Cos, Rhodes, Patara — mirrors the Aegean navigation of ch. 20 in reverse direction, now southward toward Phoenicia.
2: heurontes ploion diaperan eis Phoinikēn: at Patara, the party transfers to a larger ship sailing directly to Phoenicia, abandoning the coastal hop. The discovery of a direct-passage vessel is providential for Paul's Pentecost urgency (20:16). Phoenicia — the Syro-Lebanese coast — was evangelized by those scattered in Acts 11:19; Paul traverses communities already established.
3: anaphananteS de ten Kypron kai katalIpontes auten euōnymon: passing Cyprus to the left (south), keeping the island on the port side. Luke's nautical precision — eualōnymon (left side) is the sailor's bearing — is consistent throughout the sea-voyage sections. Tyre is identified as the unloading destination; the ship's commercial cargo determines the port of call that shapes Paul's pastoral itinerary.
4: anesantes tous mathetas emeINamen autou hemeras hepta... hoitines tō pneumati elegon tō Paulō mē epibainein eis Hierosolyma: 'finding the disciples, we stayed seven days... who through the Spirit were telling Paul not to go up to Jerusalem'. The Tyre disciples' Spirit-inspired warning creates the exegetical tension: the Spirit in 20:22-23 compels Paul toward Jerusalem; the Spirit through these disciples urges avoidance. The resolution is that the prophecy accurately disclosed danger (fulfilled) without constituting a divine prohibition — the disciples' mē was their pastoral application of the prophetic data, not the Spirit's direct command.
5: ote de egeneto hemas exartisai tas hemeras (hote de egeneto hēmas exartisai tas hēmeras): 'when our days there were completed'. Exartizō (complete/fit out) has a finishing-work sense — the seven days in Tyre were a purposeful stay, not an accident. The departure scene mirrors Miletus: the whole community, wives and children included, escorted Paul to the shore. The beach prayer — kneeling together at the water's edge — is the image of the Pauline churches at their most tender.
6: aspAsamenos allēlous epebemen eis to ploion: the mutual farewell embrace and embarkation. Aspázomai (greet/embrace) is the Christian greeting-word that frames community encounters throughout Acts. The Tyre disciples 'returned to their own homes' (eis ta idia) — the farewell is formal and final; they returned to ordinary life while Paul continued toward the extraordinary destination.
7: hēmeis de ton ploun dianysantes apo Tyrou katerchomen eis Ptolemaida: Ptolemais (modern Akko/Acre) is the last major port before Caesarea. 'One day' stop — the brevity suggests a less established community there; Paul greets and departs in the time a ship takes to unload and reload. The laconic notation preserves the travel diary's detail without elaborating what was clearly a brief pastoral touch.
8: eis Kaisareian kai eiselthontes eis ton oikon Philippou tou euangelistou (eis Kaisareian kai eiselthontes eis ton oikon Philippou tou euangelistou): Philip 'the evangelist' — the title distinguishes him from the apostle Philip. He was one of the Seven deacons (Acts 6:5), the first to bring the gospel to Samaria (8:5-8), the evangelist of the Ethiopian eunuch (8:26-39). His Caesarean household has been established since at least 10:1 (Cornelius's conversion); Philip has apparently settled there. Euangelistēs appears in Eph 4:11 and 2 Tim 4:5 as a distinct ministry-category.
9: touto de ēsan thygateres tessares parthenoi prophēteuousai: 'this man had four virgin daughters who prophesied'. Propheteuousai (prophesying, present participle) indicates ongoing prophetic ministry, not a one-time gift. Luke records the fact without describing their ministry — the four daughters fulfill the Joel 2:28 promise of daughters prophesying (Acts 2:17). Their presence in Caesarea makes Philip's household a prophetic center, which makes Agabus's arrival (v.10) a convergence of prophetic voices.
10: ēlthen tis apo tes Ioudaias prophētes onomati Agabos: Agabus last appeared in Acts 11:28 prophesying the Claudius famine; here he reappears with a personal prophecy for Paul. The two Agabus prophecies bracket Paul's missionary journeys: the first (famine) prompted the collection that Paul now carries to Jerusalem; the second announces the binding that awaits him. Agabus functions as the Jeremianic prophet who speaks the difficult word to God's servant.
11: aron ten zōnēn tou Paulou, desas heautou tous podas kai tas cheiras (arōn tēn zōnēn tou Paulou, dēsas heautou tous podas kai tas cheiras): the prophetic sign-act — binding himself with Paul's belt — echoes OT prophetic dramatization (Isa 20; Jer 13; Ezek 4-5). Houtōs dēsousin hoi Ioudaioi en Hierousalēm ton andra: 'thus the Jews in Jerusalem will bind the man'. The prophecy is specific and accurate (Acts 21:33). The sign-act makes the word visible; Agabus embodies in his own person what Paul will endure.
12: eklIomen hēmeis te kai hoi entopIoi tou mē anabainein auton eis Hierousalem: 'we ourselves and the local people begged him not to go up to Jerusalem'. Eklion (beg/beseech) is the emotional appeal-word. The community's response to Agabus's prophecy is protective — they hear the warning and try to prevent the fulfillment. Paul's refusal (v.13-14) distinguishes his obedience to divine necessity from pastoral self-preservation, the same tension that characterized Jesus's own Jerusalem journey.
13: ti poieite klaiOntes kai sykryptOntes mou ten kardian (ti poieite klaiontes kai synkryptontes mou tēn kardian): 'what are you doing weeping and breaking my heart?' Synkryptō (crush/shatter together) — a compound expressing the pain of communal grief pressing in on Paul's resolve. His declaration: egō gar ou monon dethēnai alla kai apothanein eis Hierousalem hetoimōs echō hyper tou onomatos tou kyriou Iēsou: 'I am ready not only to be bound but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus'. The martyr-vocabulary (hetoimōs echō, ready; apothanein, to die) frames Paul as the Suffering Servant-pattern apostle.
14: mē peithomenou de autou hesychAsamen eIpontes, Ho tou kyriou thelēma ginesthō: 'since he would not be persuaded, we kept quiet, saying, Let the will of the Lord be done'. Hēsychazō (become quiet/cease) — the community's acceptance. Tou kyriou thelēma ginesthō is the echo of the Lord's Prayer (genēthētō to thelēma sou, thy will be done) and Gethsemane — the shape of Christian submission. Luke presents Paul's community as learning through this episode what Jesus modeled: the will of God is not circumvented but embraced.
15: episkeusAmenoi anebainomen eis Hierousalēm (episkeuasamenoi anebainomen eis Hierousalēm): 'having prepared/packed, we went up to Jerusalem'. Episkyeuázomai (prepare/equip for travel) — the practical preparation that follows the spiritual resolution. The 'we' narrator continues; the community from Caesarea accompanies Paul, including Mnason of Cyprus (v.16), a disciple from the earliest days of the church.
16: Mnasōni tini Kyprīō archaiō mathētē: 'Mnason, a certain Cypriot, an early disciple'. Archaios mathētēs (old/early disciple) — one who was converted in the earliest days of the Jerusalem church, possibly from Pentecost or shortly after. Cyprus was the origin of Barnabas (4:36) and the location of early mission activity (13:4-12). Mnason's household in Jerusalem provides a safe base for a party whose status in the Jerusalem community is about to be tested.
17: genomenōn de hēmōn eis Hierousalēm asmenos apedexanto hēmas hoi adelphoi: 'when we arrived in Jerusalem, the brothers received us gladly'. Asmenōs (gladly/warmly) signals genuine hospitality — the arrival is welcomed, setting the scene for the James meeting of the following day. The 'we' narrator is still present. The journey that began in Acts 19:21 reaches its destination; Jerusalem is both the end of the third journey and the beginning of the passion-sequence parallels.
18: eiseiei de ho Paulos syn hēmin pros Iakōbon: the formal meeting with James and all the elders of the Jerusalem church. James is the acknowledged single leader of the Jerusalem community (15:13-21; Gal 2:12); the elders are the collegial body with him. The meeting mirrors the Acts 15 council structure. Paul's report — in sequence, all that God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry — is the same missionary reporting pattern as 14:27 and 15:4.
19: eksēgeito kath hen hekaston hōn epoiēsen ho theos en tois ethnesin dia tēs diakonias autou: 'he related one by one what God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry'. Exēgeomai (narrate in detail/expound) — the same word Luke uses for the Emmaus disciples' account (Luke 24:35). Paul's report attributes every result to God (ho theos), not his own effectiveness; the dia tes diakonias autou (through his ministry) is the instrument, not the agent. The Jerusalem elders hear a Gentile mission summary they could only partially know from hearsay.
20: hoi de akousantes edoxazon ton theon: 'when they heard, they glorified God'. The reaction to Paul's report mirrors 11:18's response to Peter's Cornelius account: when God's work is rightly reported, the proper response is doxology. The elders immediately follow with pastoral intelligence: tens of thousands of believing Jews are zealous for the law. The juxtaposition sets up the tension — Gentile-mission success alongside Torah-observant Jewish Christianity. Both exist; both glorify God; the accommodation question must be managed.
21: katēchēthēsan de peri sou hoti apostasian didasKeis apo Mōuseōs tous kata ta ethnē pantas Ioudaious: 'they have been informed about you that you teach all the Jews among the Gentiles to forsake Moses'. The charge is a misrepresentation of Paul's actual teaching (he never told Jewish believers to stop circumcising their children as a cultural practice) but reflects the rhetorical damage done by opponents. The elders are managing both Paul's real position and its popular distortion — a classic pastoral-diplomatic challenge.
22: ti oun estin: 'what then is to be done?' The Jerusalem elders' direct question acknowledges the pastoral problem without accusation. Their proposed solution — Paul's participation in a Nazirite vow-completion rite (vv.23-24) — is a public gesture of Torah-identification designed to correct the misimpression. The proposal is practical and politically realistic; it does not require Paul to abandon his Gentile-mission theology, only to demonstrate that Jewish-Christian Torah-observance and Gentile freedom coexist within the one gospel.
23: eisin hēmin andres tessares eucHen echontes eph heautōn: four men completing Nazirite vows (Num 6). The rite required shaving the head and offering prescribed sacrifices at the temple's completion — expensive enough that wealthy patrons often covered the costs for those who had vowed. Paul's participation as patron for all four demonstrates both his solidarity with Torah-observant Jewish Christianity and his material use of the collection-funds he has brought.
24: stoicHeis kai autos phylassōn ton nomon (stoicheis kai autos phylassōn ton nomon): 'you yourself also walk orderly, keeping the law'. Stoicheō (walk in step with / conform to an order) — Paul is asked to perform what is true of him: he does not require Torah-observance from Gentiles but neither does he renounce it as a Jewish man. The Apostolic Decree (v.25) is referenced as the settled Gentile policy, distinguishing it from what is asked of Paul as a Jew.
25: peri de ton pepisteukoton ethnōn hēmeis epesteilamen krinantes phulassesthai autous: the Jerusalem elders reaffirm the Apostolic Decree (15:28-29) — no Torah-burden on Gentile believers except the four abstentions (idol food, blood, strangled meat, sexual immorality). The restatement here serves to clarify that Paul's participation in the vow rite is not a reversal of Gentile freedom; the two communities have different but equally valid practices within the one church.
26: tote ho Paulos paralabōn tous andras tē echomene hēmera syn autois hagnistheis eisēei eis to hieron: Paul's participation in the purification rites and temple presentation. Hagnizō (purify) follows the LXX pattern for Nazirite completion (Num 6:9-12). Paul's physical presence in the temple — the last time in Acts — is both his pastoral accommodation and his fatal exposure. The vow-completion brings him into contact with Asian Jews who recognize him.
27: hoi apo tes Asias Ioudaioi... suneHeon panta ton ochlon kai epebalon ep auton tas cheiras: the Asian Jews — likely from Ephesus — who recognize Paul as the synagogue-disturber of their region. Synecheon (stirred up / threw into confusion) — the same verb for the Ephesian riot (19:32). Epebalon ep' auton tas cheiras (laid hands on him) — the arrest is mob action, not legal process. The accusation structure mirrors the charges against Stephen (6:13-14): temple, people, Torah.
28: Andreson Israelitai, boētheite... houtos estin ho anthrōpos ho kata tou laou kai tou nomou kai tou topou toutou didaskon pantas pantachou: 'Men of Israel, help! — this is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against the people and the law and this place'. The three charges are the Jerusalem establishment's version of the Asian Jews' accusation. The third charge echoes the Hellenist accusation against Stephen (6:13) almost verbatim; Luke's parallel is intentional — Paul's passion-sequence mirrors Stephen's.
29: ēsan gar proheōrakotes Trophimon ton Ephesion en tē polei syn autō: the error behind the temple-defilement charge — the Asian Jews had seen Trophimus the Ephesian with Paul in the city and assumed he had brought him into the temple. Proheōraō (have seen previously) — eyewitness evidence drawn to a false conclusion. The irony is that Paul was in the temple demonstrating his Torah-observance; the very act of piety they now misrepresent as temple-defilement.
30: ekinēthē te hē polis holē: 'the whole city was stirred up'. Kineomai (set in motion) — the mob-energy that characterized the Ephesian riot now repeats in Jerusalem. Synchedromē tou laou (the people ran together) — the spontaneous mob-gathering. The temple doors are shut behind Paul (ekleisthēsan hai thyrai) — the symbolic closure: the temple that Paul sought to honor by the vow-rite now bars him out. The exclusion from temple is the beginning of his path toward Rome.
31: zētountōn de auton apokteinai anebē phasis tō chiliarchō tēs speiras hoti holē syngechytai Hierousalēm: the Roman tribune receives word that all Jerusalem is in confusion. The providential timing — the Roman garrison was stationed in the Antonia Fortress adjacent to the temple precisely to manage such disturbances — places Paul under Roman protection at the moment of maximum danger. The governor-apparatus that will frustrate justice in chs. 24-26 is simultaneously Paul's protection from mob execution.
32: hos exautēs paralabōn stratiōtas kai hekatontarchas katedRamen ep autous: 'who immediately took soldiers and centurions and ran down to them'. The Roman response — soldiers and centurions plural — reflects the seriousness of the riot. Exautēs (immediately, at once) and katadrameō (run down) convey urgency. The mob stops beating Paul on sight of the soldiers; Roman military presence enforces a peace the temple establishment failed to maintain.
33: tote eggisas ho chiliarchos epelAbeto autou kai ekeleusen dethēnai halysesi dysi: 'then the tribune approached and arrested him and ordered him bound with two chains'. Halysei dysi (two chains) — Agabus's prophecy (v.11: 'thus the Jews in Jerusalem will bind the man') is fulfilled in the letter. The double chaining may reflect Roman procedure for a prisoner of uncertain legal status, but Luke marks it as prophetic fulfillment. The tribune assumes Paul is a dangerous criminal (v.38).
34: alla men ti eboōn en tō ochlō: 'but some in the crowd were shouting one thing and some another'. The cacophony of conflicting accusations prevents the tribune from establishing facts. Mē dynamenos de gnōnai to asphales (unable to determine the truth) — the Roman judicial system is overwhelmed by the mob's noise. Paul is ordered to the barracks (eis tēn parembolēn) for interrogation under controlled conditions — procedurally correct but helpless before the crowd's confusion.
35: hote de egeneto epi tous anababathmons: 'when he got to the steps'. The Antonia Fortress had a grand staircase descending to the temple court — Paul is literally being carried (bastazesthai) by soldiers above the crowd pressing below. Bia tou ochlou (the violence of the crowd) — the mob's physical force required the soldiers to lift Paul to safety. The staircase becomes a pulpit (v.40): elevation that was forced becomes platform for proclamation.
36: ēkolouthei gar to plēthos tou laou krazon, Aire auton: 'the crowd of people followed, crying, Away with him!' Aire (take away/remove) is the same cry of the Jerusalem crowd at Jesus's trial (Luke 23:18; John 19:15). Luke's verbal parallel is unmistakable — Paul's passion-sequence is narrated with deliberate Jesus-parallel. The one who followed Jesus (22:3) is now addressed with the same rejection-shout as the one he persecuted.
37: Hellēnisti ginōskeis: the tribune's surprised question when Paul addresses him in Greek. The assumption was that Paul was the Egyptian revolutionary (ho Aigyptios, v.38) who had led a desert revolt with four thousand dagger-men (sikarioi). Paul's educated Greek (Hellēnisti) signals his diaspora identity; the tribune's surprise marks a status-recalibration. Greek is the language of the educated Roman world; Paul uses it to claim protected status.
38: ouk ara sy ei ho Aigyptios ho pro toutōn tōn hēmerōn anastatōsas kai exagagōn eis tēn erēmon tous tetrakischilious andras tōn sikariōn: the tribune's specific identification — the Egyptian prophet who led four thousand sikarioi (dagger-men, assassins). Josephus records this figure (Ant. 20.169-172; J.W. 2.261-263) — an Egyptian false prophet who led followers to the Mount of Olives promising the walls of Jerusalem would fall. Felix dispersed the movement; the Egyptian escaped. The tribune's mistaken identity is both realistic and ironic: Paul is the true prophet, mistaken for the false one.
39: Ioudaios eimi, Tarseus tes Kilikias, ouk asēmou poleōs politēs: 'I am a Jew, a citizen of Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of no obscure city'. Ouk asēmou poleōs (not of an obscure city) — the litotes is Paul's educated self-presentation. Tarsus was a major intellectual and commercial center, ranking with Alexandria and Athens in Strabo's assessment. Jewish citizenship in Tarsus was a recognized legal status. Paul deploys all three identities — Jewish, Tarsian, and (implicitly) Roman citizen (22:25-29) — in sequence to command respect and legal protection.
40: epistrepsamenos de kai kataseisas tē cheiri tō laō, polles de sigēs genomenēs, prosefonēsen tē Hebraidi dialektō legōn: 'turning and waving his hand to the people, and when great silence fell, he addressed them in the Hebrew dialect'. Kataseisas tē cheiri (waving the hand for silence) is the orator's call-to-attention gesture; Luke uses it for Paul at other speech-openings (13:16). Hebraidi dialektō (Hebrew/Aramaic dialect) — switching from Greek to Aramaic silences the crowd more effectively than the soldiers; language is Paul's most effective crowd-control. The speech that follows (22:1-21) is Paul's autobiography as Christian calling.
